name: Whom She Killed
shortDescription: A term used in Sumerian mythology to refer to the individuals killed by Ereshkigal, Queen of the Underworld, asserting her power and authority in the land of the dead.
description: In Sumerian mythology, the phrase 'Whom She Killed' (a more literal translation is often difficult to ascertain) represents the collective individuals who met their demise and fell under the dominion of Ereshkigal, the formidable Queen of Kur, the Sumerian Underworld. While not a deity in the traditional sense, this collective embodies Ereshkigal's power and position. Her role as ruler meant she held sway over all who entered her realm, and the act of 'killing' or causing death was integral to her identity and authority. The inhabitants of Kur were thus 'Whom She Killed', highlighting the inevitability of death and Ereshkigal's ultimate control. The phrase underscores the grim realities of the Sumerian afterlife, a dark and dusty realm where the deceased existed as shades. This concept is intertwined with the myth of Inanna's descent to the underworld, where Inanna is forced to confront Ereshkigal and ultimately suffers death, demonstrating the Queen's absolute power. The 'Whom She Killed' are not individualized in Sumerian texts; rather, they are a mass of souls under her command, a constant reminder of her deadly reign. The lack of individual identity further reinforces the bleak outlook of the Sumerian afterlife, where individuality is lost in the face of death.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
sources.author: University of Oxford

sources.title: Before Philosophy
sources.author: Henri Frankfort, H.A. Frankfort, John A. Wilson, Thorkild Jacobsen

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to Ereshkigal
worshipPractices.description: As 'Whom She Killed' are under Ereshkigal's dominion, offerings to the Queen of the Underworld indirectly acknowledge them. These offerings, usually food, drink, or symbolic items, aimed to appease Ereshkigal and ensure safe passage for the deceased and prevent negative influences from the underworld from affecting the living.

worshipPractices.name: Funerary Rites
worshipPractices.description: Elaborate funerary rituals were performed to honor the dead and prepare them for their journey to the underworld. These rites often involved lamentations, burial goods, and offerings to ensure the deceased's comfort and acceptance by Ereshkigal and the other inhabitants of Kur.

festivals.name: Kisal Festival
festivals.description: This festival, sometimes associated with appeasing deities of the underworld, likely involved remembrance of ancestors and those who had passed. Offerings and prayers were made to ensure their well-being in the afterlife, acknowledging the power of Ereshkigal and those under her control.

festivals.name: Month of Dumuzi
festivals.timing: Fourth month of the Sumerian calendar
festivals.description: While primarily focused on Dumuzi's descent to the underworld and Inanna's journey to retrieve him, the Month of Dumuzi implicitly acknowledges the realm of the dead and the power of its queen. Lamentations and rituals were performed, recognizing the inevitability of death and the transition into Ereshkigal's domain.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of 'Whom She Killed,' representing those under the dominion of the underworld, subtly resonates in modern interpretations of death and the afterlife. While not directly invoked, the idea of a realm beyond and a powerful figure presiding over the deceased continues to influence artistic and literary depictions of mortality and the journey beyond life.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Similar to 'Whom She Killed', the Shades are the collective souls of the deceased residing in the Underworld ruled by Hades; they represent those who have fallen under his dominion after death.

cross_cultural_parallels.entity: The Souls of the Dead in Duat
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: These souls, dwelling in the Duat (Underworld), are judged and either pass into the afterlife or face annihilation; like 'Whom She Killed', they are a collective of those who have died and entered the realm of the dead, facing its trials and ruler (Osiris).

These are the souls of those who died of sickness or old age, residing in Helheim, ruled by Hel. They represent the masses who did not die in battle and thus fell under Hel's domain, mirroring the broad category of 'Whom She Killed'.

associations.name: Clay
associations.significance: Clay represents the material from which humans were formed, and thus their eventual return to the earth after death, falling under Ereshkigal's domain.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black symbolizes the darkness of the Underworld, Kur, and the absence of life, representing the fate of those 'Whom She Killed'.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven, a sacred number in Sumerian culture, might represent the totality of souls or the seven gates of the Underworld they passed through.

associations.name: Setting Sun
associations.significance: The setting sun mirrors the journey of life ending and descending into the darkness of the Underworld ruled by Ereshkigal.

associations.name: Mortality
associations.significance: The phrase directly embodies the concept of mortality and the inevitability of death, which brings individuals into Ereshkigal's realm.

associations.name: Vulture
associations.significance: The vulture, a carrion bird, represents death and decay, reflecting the state of those who have fallen under Ereshkigal's power.

associations.name: Willow
associations.significance: The willow, often associated with mourning and weeping, symbolizes the sorrow and lament associated with death and loss in Sumerian culture.

companions.name: Namtar
companions.description: Namtar is Ereshkigal's chief minister and messenger. He carries out her decrees and spreads disease and misfortune.

companions.name: Neti
companions.description: Neti is the gatekeeper of Kur, the Sumerian underworld. He guards the seven gates and ensures that only those permitted by Ereshkigal may enter.

companions.name: Gidim
companions.description: Gidim are the spirits of the dead, residing in Kur under Ereshkigal's rule. They form a large part of 'Whom She Killed'.

companions.name: Allatu
companions.description: Allatu represents a primordial form of Ereshkigal, embodying the power and terror of the underworld. She protects the realm and enforces its laws.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Association with Ereshkigal
extendedContent.content:
The concept of 'Whom She Killed' isn't a birth myth but rather a consequence of death itself within Sumerian cosmology. It represents the countless souls who transitioned from the land of the living to Kur, the desolate underworld ruled by Ereshkigal. Unlike deities born from primordial chaos or divine unions, these individuals are defined by their shared fate: death and subjugation to Ereshkigal's authority. The Lament for Dumuzi provides glimpses into the somber existence within Kur, where these deceased individuals reside, often depicted as shadows or echoes of their former selves. 

The connection between 'Whom She Killed' and Ereshkigal is absolute. Their existence within the Sumerian worldview is entirely dependent on her role as the Queen of the Underworld. She is the gatekeeper, judge, and ruler of this realm, and all who enter become subject to her dominion. Her power is further amplified by the Anunnaki, the judges of the underworld, who assist her in maintaining order and dispensing judgment upon the dead.

Scholars interpret this collective as a reflection of Sumerian anxieties about mortality and the afterlife. The harsh and bleak depiction of Kur suggests a pessimistic outlook on death, where individuality is diminished, and existence is reduced to a shadowy, joyless state under Ereshkigal's control. This contrasts with other ancient cultures where the afterlife was often envisioned as a continuation of earthly life or a journey to a more desirable realm.

extendedContent.title: Absence of Worship and Temples
extendedContent.content:
Unlike other deities in the Sumerian pantheon, 'Whom She Killed' were not directly worshipped, nor were temples erected in their honor. This is because they represent the passive recipients of death, not active agents possessing divine power. While offerings and rituals were performed to appease Ereshkigal, the Queen of the Underworld, these actions were aimed at preventing misfortune and ensuring the safe passage of the deceased, rather than venerating the dead themselves as a collective entity. The primary focus of religious practice was on the gods who could influence life and death, not those who had already succumbed to it.

The absence of direct worship also stems from the perceived state of the dead in Kur. They were viewed as powerless and dependent on the living for sustenance and remembrance. Rituals like the kispu, funerary feasts held to provide nourishment for the deceased, underscore this belief. However, these feasts were not directed at 'Whom She Killed' as a unified group, but rather at specific ancestors or deceased family members.

The lack of dedicated temples or cults for the deceased highlights the hierarchical structure of Sumerian religion. Power and agency resided with the deities, while the dead occupied a liminal space, subject to the whims of the gods and the memories of the living. Therefore, religious practices were primarily focused on maintaining relationships with the divine beings who controlled the forces of nature and human destiny.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content:
Because 'Whom She Killed' represents a collective of deceased individuals rather than a distinct deity, it lacks a specific iconography in Sumerian art. There are no visual depictions that directly portray this entity. Instead, representations of death and the Underworld in Sumerian art offer indirect insights into how they were conceived. Scenes depicting Ereshkigal on her throne in Kur, often surrounded by demons and the Anunnaki judges, provide a symbolic context for understanding the fate of the deceased.

Cylinder seals and reliefs frequently depict funerary rituals, offering scenes, and processions to the Underworld. These images show individuals presenting offerings to appease the gods or mourning the loss of loved ones. While these scenes do not explicitly depict the deceased, they convey the emotional and cultural significance of death in Sumerian society. Images of demons, such as those associated with sickness and death, also hint at the dangers and anxieties surrounding the transition to the Underworld.

Furthermore, textual descriptions of Kur in myths like 'Inanna's Descent to the Underworld' paint a vivid picture of the bleak and desolate realm where the deceased reside. These descriptions, while not visual representations, contribute to the overall understanding of the fate of 'Whom She Killed' – a shadowy existence within Ereshkigal's domain, devoid of joy and hope. Therefore, understanding the artistic and literary portrayals of death and the Underworld is crucial for grasping the concept of 'Whom She Killed' in Sumerian mythology.

extendedContent.title: Role in the Cosmic Order and Ereshkigal's Power
extendedContent.content:
The existence of 'Whom She Killed' is intrinsically linked to the Sumerian understanding of the cosmic order and, more specifically, to Ereshkigal's role within it. The Sumerians believed in a tripartite cosmos consisting of the heavens ruled by Anu, the earth inhabited by humans, and the underworld (Kur) governed by Ereshkigal. The constant influx of souls into Kur, represented by 'Whom She Killed', reinforced Ereshkigal's position as a vital, albeit dreaded, component of this cosmic structure. Her dominion over the dead ensured the cyclical nature of life and death, a fundamental aspect of the Sumerian worldview.

Ereshkigal's power derived directly from her control over the underworld and its inhabitants. The sheer number of souls under her command, represented by 'Whom She Killed', symbolized her immense authority and influence. This power was not benevolent; Ereshkigal was often depicted as a formidable and unforgiving queen, ruling her realm with an iron fist. Her role was not to nurture or comfort the dead, but rather to maintain order and prevent them from interfering with the world of the living.

The concept of 'Whom She Killed' also serves as a reminder of the limitations of human existence and the inevitability of death. It underscores the Sumerian belief that even the most powerful and influential individuals ultimately succumb to death and become subject to Ereshkigal's rule. This awareness of mortality likely influenced their religious practices and their attempts to appease the gods, hoping to secure a more favorable fate in the afterlife, even if that afterlife was ultimately a shadowy existence in Kur.
